title: Feature request: INCLUDE instruction
body:
### Feature request description

Sometimes when using devcontainers, you want a base image for common tools like vim, and then separate child images for say each programming language (C++, Rust, etc.). It is tedious to actually build this base image, since you basically have to reconstruct the `podman build` command that VS Code is running when you use `Dev Containers: Rebuild Container`, which it in turn constructs by parsing [the `devcontainer.json` file](https://containers.dev/implementors/json_reference/). The actual command can be quite complex because devcontainers support many niche features. This is an actual example from the build logs of the devcontainer of a project I am working on:

```log
[3394 ms] Start: Run: podman ps -q -a --filter label=devcontainer.local_folder=/home/rdong/projects/cpp_project --filter label=devcontainer.config_file=/home/rdong/projects/cpp_project/.devcontainer/devcontainer.json
[3435 ms] Start: Run: podman inspect --type image docker.io/fedora:latest
[3482 ms] Start: Run: podman buildx build --load --build-arg BUILDKIT_INLINE_CACHE=1 -f /tmp/devcontainercli-rdong/container-features/0.86.1-1778165339054/Dockerfile-with-features -t vsc-cpp_project-1a917c6c7f2b3d2b5bde19199f130d1c7d9f5a68898e8780fcb310393af681df --target dev_containers_target_stage --build-arg REMOTE_USER=dev --build-arg HOST_UID=1000 --build-arg HOST_GID=1000 --build-arg _DEV_CONTAINERS_BASE_IMAGE=dev_container_auto_added_stage_label /home/rdong/projects/cpp_project/.devcontainer/Containerfiles
```

Furthermore, as far as I can tell, there is no way to obtain this build command unless you actually run the build and get it from the logs.

### Suggest potential solution

I am proposing an `INCLUDE` instruction that just essentially performs textual inclusion of another Containerfile in a similar manner to `#include` in C/C++ or `include` in Make. So for instance, you could do this:

`tools.Containerfile`:

```dockerfile
FROM docker.io/debian:latest
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y vim
```

`cpp.Containerfile`:

```dockerfile
INCLUDE path/to/tools.Containerfile
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y clang
```

`rust.Containerfile`:

```dockerfile
INCLUDE path/to/tools.Containerfile
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y rust-all
```

The benefit here is that you can just point your `devcontainer.json` file to `cpp.Containerfile` or `rust.Containerfile`, and VS Code with build it the normal way, which in the process will build the "base image" with the exact same flags/configuration. 

### Have you considered any alternatives?

Using CMake `configure_file` to do a C-like preprocessor pass to manually paste the base Containerfile's contents into the child Containerfile. 

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
podman build and buildah already support running the cpp preprocessor on Contianerfiles.

https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-build.1.html#description

> Containerfiles ending with a “.in” suffix are preprocessed via CPP(1). This can be useful to decompose Containerfiles into several reusable parts that can be used via CPP’s #include directive. Containerfiles ending in .in are restricted to no comment lines unless they are CPP commands. Note, a Containerfile.in file can still be used by other tools when manually preprocessing them via cpp -E.
